coredata.dc:title: Caffeic acid-grafted chitosan/sodium alginate/nanoclay-based multifunctional 3D-printed hybrid scaffolds for local drug release therapy after breast cancer surgery
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Ya
Liu, Yaqian
Hu, Xueyan
Lu, Yueqi

Jin, Wenbo
Liu, Wang
Shu, Yan
Cheng, Yuen Yee

Song, Kedong
coredata.dc:description: Breast cancer is one of the most common malignant tumors in women all over the world. Mastectomy is the most effective treatment, but there are serious problems such as high tumor recurrence rate and side effects of chemotherapy. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a therapeutic strategy that can effectively promote postoperative wound healing and inhibit local tumor recurrence. In this study, a 3D printing scaffold based on carbon dots-curcumin nano-drug release (CCNPs) was developed as a local drug delivery platform (named CCNACA using CCNPs, Sodium alginate, Nanoclay and Caffeic Acid grafted Chitosan as raw materials), which has the ability to visualize drug release. The 14-day drug release test in vitro showed that the tumor inhibition rate of CCNACA scaffolds on breast cancer cells (MCF-7) was 73.77 ± 1.68 %. And the CCNACA scaffolds had good long-term antibacterial (Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus) activity. Animal experiments have shown that implanting CCNACA scaffolds into surgical defects can inhibit postoperative residual cancer cells, reduce inflammation, promote angiogenesis, and repair tissue defects caused by surgery. In summary, the local drug delivery system of this manuscript has great potential in wound healing and prevention of tumor recurrence after breast cancer surgery.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical properties of CDs. a) the absorption spectra of CDs aqueous solution at different concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL). Fluorescence spectra of CDs at different concentrations with b) 25, c) 50, d) 100, e) 200 and f) 400 μg/mL.

Morphology and luminescence properties of CD, Cur and CCNPs. a) The relationship between maximum emission wavelength and concentration of CDs. b) Fluorescence spectrum of Cur in DMF. c) Fluorescence spectrum of CCNPs in aqueous solutions. d) Absorption spectra of CDs, Cur and CurNPs. e) FTIR spectra of CDs (black solid line), Cur (red solid line) and CCNPs (blue solid line). f) Full scan XPS spectra of CDs (black solid line), Cur (red solid line) and CCNPs (blue solid line).

The morphology and stability of CDs and CCNPs. a) TEM image of CDs, scale bar: 20 nm. Inset: size distribution of CDs determined by Image J, and HR-TEM image of CDs. b) TEM image of CCNPs, scale bar: 500 nm. c) Area enlarged view of b). Inset: size distribution of CCNPs determined by Image J. c) DLS of CCNPs images. d) Diameter and PDI changes of CurNPs in e) aqueous medium and f) 10 % FBS for 14 days.

Endocytosis and toxicity testing of CDs, Cur and CCNPs. Two-photon laser confocal images of MCF-7 cells incubated with a) CDs, b) Cur and c) CCNPs at different concentrations of 50, 100, 200 μg/mL for 6 h, respectively. Scale bar, 20 μm. Quantification of fluorescence intensity of d) CDs, e) Cur and f) CCNPs under different laser conditions using Image J software. g) Cytotoxicity of different concentrations of CDs on MCF-7/3 T3 cells. Data indicated by mean ± SD. N.S.: p > 0.05, *: p < 0.05, **: p < 0.01, ***: p < 0.001.

Cytotoxicity test of CDs, Cur and CCNPs. Cytotoxicity of different concentrations of a) Cur and b) CCNPs on 3 T3/MCF-7 cells. The fluorescence picture of MCF-7 cells treated with 300 μg/mL of c) CDs, d) Cur and e) CCNPs with calcium-AM (AM, green, live cells) and propidium iodide (PI, red, dead cells). The fluorescence picture of 3 T3 cells treated with 300 μg/mL of f) CDs, g) Cur and h) CCNPs with calcium-AM (AM, green, live cells) and propidium iodide (PI, red, dead cells). Scale bar: 200 μm. Data indicated by mean ± SD. N.S.: p > 0.05, *: p < 0.05, **: p < 0.01, ***: p < 0.001.

Rheological properties testing of NACA/CCNACA inks. a) Viscosity versus temperature curve of ink in the range of 10– 40 °C. b) G′ and G″ of ink versus temperature in the range of 10∼40°C. c) G′ and G″ of ink versus angular frequency curve. d) Viscosity of the ink versus shear rate. e) Thixotropy of the NACA/CCNACA ink. f) Compression performance of 3D printed NACA/CCNACA scaffolds.

Physical Properties Characterization of 3D NACA/CCNACA scaffolds. a) Photo of NACA ink and b) 3D printing NACA scaffolds. c) Photo of CCNACA ink and d) 3D printing CCNACA scaffolds. e) SEM picture of NACA scaffolds, scale bar: 100 μm. f) SEM local enlarged picture of NACA scaffolds, scale bar: 100 μm. g) SEM picture of CCNACA scaffold, scale bar: 100 μm. h) SEM local enlarged picture of CCNACA scaffolds, scale bar: 100 μm. Energy spectrum picture and EDS elemental characterization of a region of i) NACA and j) CCNACA scaffolds.

Evaluation of oxidation resistance of materials. OD value of DPPH radical treated by a) CDs, Cur, CCNPs, b) CS, CA, CSCA, c) NACA, CCNACA. Efficiency of DPPH radical scavenging by d) CDs, Cur, CCNPs, e) CS, CA, CSCA, f) NACA, CCNACA. OD value of ABTS radical treated by g) CDs, Cur, CCNPs, h) CS, CA, CSCA, i) NACA, CCNACA. Efficiency of ABTS radical scavenging by j) CDs, Cur, CCNPs, k) CS, CA, CSCA, i) NACA, CCNACA. Detection of ROS in m) 3 T3 and n) MCF-7 cells incubated with CDs Cur, CCNPs, CS, CA, CSCA, NACA and CCNACA. Scale bar: 200 μm.

Cell proliferation testing of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds. Pictures of 3 T3 cells proliferating on a) NACA and b) CCNACA scaffolds and 3D reconstruction of cell scaffolds formed from laser scan images along the z-axis at different times (1, 3, 7, 14 days), scale bar: 200 μm. Pictures of MCF-7 cells proliferating on c) NACA and d)CCNACA scaffolds and 3D reconstruction of cell scaffolds formed from laser scan images along the z-axis at different times (1, 3, 7, 14 days), scale bar: 200 μm.

SEM test of cells (3 T3/MCF-7) on scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA) for different days (1, 3, 7, 14 days). SEM images of a) 3 T3 and b) MCF-7 cells on NACA scaffolds at different culture time (1, 3, 7, 14 day). Among them, lines a2 and b2 (Scale bar: 10 μm) are partial enlarged images of the pictures of lines a1 and b1 (Scale bar: 100 μm) respectively. SEM images of c) 3 T3 and d) MCF-7 cells on CCNACA scaffolds at different culture time (1, 3, 7, 14 day). Among them, lines c2 and c2 (Scale bar: 10 μm) are partial enlarged images of the pictures of lines c1 and c1 (Scale bar: 100 μm) respectively.

Evaluation of drug release with CCNACA scaffolds. Cumulative release curves of a) CCNPs and b) CCNACA scaffolds in pH = 7.4, pH = 6.5 and pH = 5.0 buffer solution incubated at 37 °C. c) CCK assay of cells (3 T3/MCF-7) was used to evaluate drug release from CCNACA scaffolds at different culture time (1, 3, 7, 14 days, respectively). Live/dead cell staining assay of cells (3 T3/MCF-7) was used to evaluate drug release from CCNACA scaffolds at d) day 1, e) day 3, f) day 7 and g) day 14. Data indicated by mean ± SD. N.S.: p > 0.05, *: p < 0.05, **: p < 0.01, ***: p < 0.001. Scale bar: 200 μm.

Antibacterial test of CCNACA scaffolds. Qualitative tests of the antibacterial ability of samples (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CSCA, NACA, CCNACA) to a) S. aureus (Gram-positive) and b) E. coli (Gram-negative) at different time (1, 3, 7 day).

Antibacterial test of CCNACA scaffolds. OD value tests of the antibacterial ability of samples (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CSCA, NACA, CCNACA) to S. aureus and E. coli at a) 1, b) 3 and c) 7 day. Survival ratio of bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli) with samples (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CSCA, NACA, CCNACA) at d) 1, e) 3 and f) 7 day. g) Bacteriostatic zone test of samples (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CSCA, NACA, CCNACA) against bacteria (S. aureus and E. coli) at different time (day 1, day 3 and day 7, respectively).

The study of CCNACA scaffolds to promote postoperative wound healing and imaging. a) Wound healing pictures of mice treated in control group, NACA and CCNACA group for different days (3, 7, 14 days). b) In vivo fluorescence imaging of mice treated in control group, NACA group and CCNACA group for different days (3, 7, 14 days). c) Quantitative analysis of the mean fluorescence signal of the wound site over time (3, 7, 14 days). Elisa test for d) TNF-α factor and e) IL-10 factor.

Histological staining analysis of wound in different groups. At the 3, 7 and 14 day of postoperative wound treatment, b) H&E tissue staining, c) Masson tissue staining, d) CD68 immunohistological staining and e) CD31 immunohistological staining were performed in different treatment groups (control group, NACA group and CCNACA group), respectively.

Schematic diagram of CCNACA scaffolds for postoperative treatment of breast cancer and antibacterial performance evaluation. a) Preparation of CDs and CCNPs. b) Preparation of CCNACA scaffolds and their antibacterial properties in vitro. c) Research of CCNACA scaffolds on wound healing, tumor recurrence and angiogenesis after tumor implantation in mice.

head.ce:title: Caffeic acid-grafted chitosan/sodium alginate/nanoclay-based multifunctional 3D-printed hybrid scaffolds for local drug release therapy after breast cancer surgery

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Breast cancer is one of the most common malignant tumors in women all over the world. Mastectomy is the most effective treatment, but there are serious problems such as high tumor recurrence rate and side effects of chemotherapy. Therefore, there is an urgent need for a therapeutic strategy that can effectively promote postoperative wound healing and inhibit local tumor recurrence. In this study, a 3D printing scaffold based on carbon dots-curcumin nano-drug release (CCNPs) was developed as a local drug delivery platform (named CCNACA using CCNPs, Sodium alginate, Nanoclay and Caffeic Acid grafted Chitosan as raw materials), which has the ability to visualize drug release. The 14-day drug release test in vitro showed that the tumor inhibition rate of CCNACA scaffolds on breast cancer cells (MCF-7) was 73.77 ± 1.68 %. And the CCNACA scaffolds had good long-term antibacterial (Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus) activity. Animal experiments have shown that implanting CCNACA scaffolds into surgical defects can inhibit postoperative residual cancer cells, reduce inflammation, promote angiogenesis, and repair tissue defects caused by surgery. In summary, the local drug delivery system of this manuscript has great potential in wound healing and prevention of tumor recurrence after breast cancer surgery.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Breast cancer is the most commonly diagnosed type of cancer in women and the leading cause of cancer death in women worldwide (Chen et al., 2019; Jia et al., 2020). At present, surgery is still the main form of intervention in comprehensive breast cancer treatment. Breast-conserving surgery, which can remove the primary breast tumor and adjacent breast tissue while preserving breast shape, has been considered an ideal surgical alternative to mastectomy for patients with early-stage breast cancer (Kennedy et al., 2020; Pradipta et al., 2020). The residual tumor cells could remind dormant but has the potential to resume growing, this could lead to difficult wound healing and tumor recurrence. Therefore, some chemotherapy drugs, such as curcumin (Cur) or paclitaxel, are usually used to prevent tumor regrow (Patel et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). However, the low efficient of chemotherapy, drug resistance, side effects, which brings pain to patients. It is an urgent requirement to design a compound treatment platform that can effectively carry drugs, inhibit tumor recurrence and facilitate surgical trauma.

In recent years, with the development of nanotechnology, drug delivery systems (NDSS) based on nano carriers have aroused great interest in cancer treatment. Compared with anticancer drugs without carriers, NDSS has the ability to improve solubility and biocompatibility of chemotherapy drugs, and enhance cell internalization and tumor accumulation by increase the permeability and retention (EPR) effect (Chen, Luo, & Zhang, 2019; Janmohammadi et al., 2023; Raj et al., 2021; van der Meel et al., 2019). It is worth mentioning that carbon dots (CDs) have become more popular nanomaterials in recent years due to their excellent luminescent properties, strong hydrophilicity, good biocompatibility, low toxicity and low-cost to synthesis. Most importantly, the surface of CDs can easily be modified with surface functional groups (COOH, NH2, etc.) that allows good water solubility and can be used as nanocarriers to combine with other materials through covalent or non-covalent interactions (Du et al., 2019; Liu, Li, & Yang, 2021; Chung, Revia, & Zhang, 2021; Lu et al., 2021; Nasrin, Hassan, & Gomes, 2020). For example, Zheng et al. used polychromatic light-emitting CDs as drug carriers to synthesize ultra small size diagnostic and therapeutic nano drugs (CDs-Oxa) through covalent coupling with the anti-tumor chemotherapy drug oxaliplatin (Oxa). CDs-Oxa can simultaneously release CDs and platinum drugs to specific locations and organs, enabling integrated research in monitoring and treating diseases (Zheng et al., 2014). Tang et al. used the direct surface coupling strategy to make the CDs surface loaded with Dox to form a drug delivery system (FRET-CDot DDS). Among them, the FRET effect between CDs and Dox enables the drug delivery system to carry out fluorescence imaging and has excellent targeting delivery and drug release capabilities for cancer cells (Tang et al., 2013). Sun et al. synthesized amino-rich red light-emitting CDs, and combined the above CDs as a carrier with chlorin (Ce6) to form nanoparticle Ce6-RCDs. The results showed that Ce6-RCDs had good photothermal therapy (PTT) and photodynamic therapy (PDT) effects. And Ce6-RCDs also have fluorescence imaging and photoacoustic imaging effects, which can be used for biological multimodal imaging (Sun et al., 2019). Functional nanomaterials are mostly used for disease diagnosis and treatment through systemic intravenous administration. However, due to the small size of nanomaterials which can be quickly removed from the circulation that prevented reaching the diseased site. This resulting in low drug utilization ratio as well as repeated administration needed.

As a comparison to large-scale chemotherapy and systemic intravenous injection, local administration can produce effective therapeutic effects and less adverse effects on normal tissues. It is important that local administration can achieve the effect of continuous drug release, and become an ideal drug delivery method for adjuvant disease treatment after surgery (Y. Zhang et al., 2020; Li et al., 2020; R. Li et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2018). Local drug delivery system based on 3D printing has received more attention in the field of tumor treatment and tissue repair. In brief, the regenerated tissue and physiological microenvironment are simulated by 3D printing technology, and drug loaded nanoparticles are embedded in the composite hydrogel ink for printing to form a composite drug loaded scaffold (Shi et al., 2020; Y. Yang et al., 2020). Currently, a number of 3D printing-based delivery systems have been developed for precise control of drug delivery (Dave, Mahmud, & Gomes, 2022; Dehghani, Ardekani, Hassan, & Gomes, 2018; Heid & Boccaccini, 2020; Nasrin, Hassan, Mirabet, Windhab, & Gomes, 2021). For example, Long et al. prepared drug-loaded 3D scaffolds by adding local anesthetic lidocaine hydrochloride (LDC) to 3D printed chitosan-pectin (CS-PEC) hydrogels. These scaffolds are suitable for absorbing exudates and maintaining a moist wound healing environment with sustained drug release effects. This study demonstrated the possibility of using 3D printing hydrogels for wound dressings (Long et al., 2019). Ajdary et al. integrated two model drugs (3I-1000 and curcumin) into a hydrogel scaffold by 3D printing technology to form a cardiac patch with sustained drug release. The results showed that the cardiac patch can be slowly degraded in vitro that prevented the sudden release of the drug (Ajdary et al., 2020). Therefore, the selection of composite scaffold materials with excellent printability, good biocompatibility, good biodegradability, no cell and tissue toxicity, anti-bacterial, anti-oxidation in one for wrapping nano drugs are the key considerations of 3D printing local drug delivery system.

It is generally known that natural polymers have the advantages of good biocompatibility, biodegradability and dose not induce toxicity at cellular level, etc., and natural polymers have become commonly used materials in drug delivery such as the hydrogel systems (Bhattarai, Gunn, & Zhang, 2010; L. Han et al., 2020; B. Yang et al., 2020). Chitosan (CS) is a naturally occurring biomacromolecule, it has attracted great interest in research during recent years. Chitosan has good biodegradability, biocompatibility, extensive antibacterial properties and can be easily modified to add functional group (Chi et al., 2020; C. Zhou et al., 2022; Liang, Li, Huang, Yu, & Guo, 2021). Wound recovery after tumor resection is a complex process, the potential of bacterial infection and inflammatory reaction at the wound site can prevent the healing process. The increase of Reactive Oxygen Species (ROS) level will lead to oxidative stress and cell damage, which will seriously inhibit the healing process (Peng et al., 2021; Li, Fu, Duan, Zhu, & Fan, 2022; D. Liu et al., 2023). Antioxidants has the potential to reduce the harmful effects, however, due to the poor water solubility of chitosan and the lack of hydrogen donor or conjugated structure molecules, its antioxidant capacity is limited. In order to expand the application of CS, researchers have attempted molecular modification by grafting copolymerization to introduce the properties of ideal materials into CS (W. Han et al., 2020; Shao et al., 2023). Studies indicated that caffeic acid (CA) has excellent antibacterial and antioxidant activities, phenolic hydroxyl in CA can form a network with inorganic or organic substrates through hydrogen bond or hydrophobic interaction. Therefore, grafting CA onto CS molecules can effectively improve the solubility of CS that improve the antioxidant and antibacterial properties (Shiu et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2019; Z. Zhou et al., 2022). For example, Lee et al. reported a nanoclay-organohydrogel bone sealant (NoBS) that integrates multiple physicochemical cues for bone regeneration into a single system. Among them, the modified CA-modified CS possesses inherent bactericidal and antioxidant activities, and acts as an intermolecular network precursor for gels with injectable and self-healing capabilities (Lee et al., 2020). In addition, in order to improve the supportability and printability of hydrogel materials, nanoclays (NC), have been widely used as inorganic nanofillers in the field of 3D printing. A number of studies have shown that NC can improve the mechanical properties of hydrogels such as hardness and toughness, thereby increasing the viscosity of printing inks and stabilizing the hydrogel network. Importantly, NC not only possess important regenerative medicine properties, such as cell adhesion and diffusion, but also have the ability to modulate drug release (Liang et al., 2020; Prasannan, Udomsin, Tsai, Wang, & Lai, 2020). Therefore, NC have excellent development prospects in drug delivery and tissue engineering scaffolds.

In this study, we prepared CDs-Cur nanoparticles (CCNPs) by nano precipitation method by using the prepared CDs as carriers and loading curcumin (Cur). Then, a 3D printed composite scaffold based on drug delivery (CCNACA) was synthesized by 3D printing technology using caffeic acid chemically grafted chitosan (CSCA), nanoclay (NC) and drug CCNPs as raw materials. Our result indicated that the composite scaffold (CCNACA) has good biocompatibility, antibacterial and antioxidant activities, and sustained drug release effect, which can effectively inhibit tumor recurrence and repair the defect area after tumor resection. The prepared CCNACA scaffolds have excellent fluorescence properties and can be used for real-time drug release tracking in vivo (Scheme 1 ). To sum up, this 3D printing composite scaffolds based on drug delivery has great development prospects and application potential in tumor local treatment and tissue repair.

Citric acid (≥99.5 %, AR, Mw: 210.14 g/mol, purchased from Xilong Science Co., Ltd., China). P-phenylenediamine (PP, ≥98.5 %, AR, Mw: 108.14 g/mol, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory). Formamide (99 %, AR, Mw: 45.04 g/mol, purchased from Sigma Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). NaCl (≥99.5 %, AR, Mw: 58.44 g/mol, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), KCl (≥99.5 %, AR, Mw: 74.55 g/mol, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), NaH2PO4·2H2O (≥99 %, AR, Mw: 156.01 g/mol, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), KH2PO4(≥99.5 %, AR, Mw: 136.09 g/mol, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory) and Na2HPO4·12H2O (≥99 %, AR, Mw: 358.14 g/mol, Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory). Deuterium oxide (D2O, 99.9%D, Mw:20.03, purchased from Shanghai HaoHONG Biomedical Technology Co., LTD). Dimethyl sulfoxide-d6 (TMS) (C2D6OS, 99.8%D, 0.03 v/v% TMS, Mw:84.17, purchased from Shanghai HaoHONG Biomedical Technology Co., LTD). Curcumin (Cur, 90 %, purchased from Henan Zhongda Hengyuan Biotechnology Co., Ltd.). N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF, ≥99.5 %, Mw: 73.09 g/mol, purchased from Xilong Science Co., Ltd.). Chitosan (viscosity: 50–200 mpa·s, Mw: 100,000 g/mol, degree of deacetylation: 80 % ∼ 90 %, Shanghai Yien Chemical Technology Co., Ltd.). Acetic acid (AR, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). Caffeic acid (CA, 98 %, Mw: 180.16 g/mol, purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.). 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-carbodiimide (EDC, Purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, Purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). 2-morpholinoethanesulfonic acid (MES, Purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). DPPH (1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl, Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd.). ABTS (Diammonium Salt, purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, St. Louis, USA). DCFH-DA (Abbkine, Wuhan Biotechnology Co., LTD.). Nanoclay (NC, Lithium magnesium silicate, Lithium soapstone, Zhejiang Fenghong New Materials Co., Ltd.). Sodium alginate (Alg, Chemically Pure (CP), Mw = 30,000 g/mol, mannuronic acid (M)/Guluronic acid (G) = 1.5, viscosity: 200 ± 20 mPa·s, pH value (10 g/L,25 °C): 6.0–8.0, moisture≤15 %, China Tianjin Kemio Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd). The viscosity and support properties of Alg were considered and subsequently correlated with specific characteristics of the 3D printed scaffold. All the reagents were kept as received condition before use, and all chemical reagents were analytically pure and could be used without further purification.

Oil bath (Gongyi Yuhua Instrument Co., Ltd., model: DF-101S) was used for the synthesis of CDs. Centrifuge were purchased from HERMLE Labortechnik GmbH (model: HERMLE Z323), and centrifuged the synthesis of CDs and CCNPs. Dialysis (purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Mw: 14 KDa/3.5 KDa) were used to remove unreacted small molecules during CDs and CCNPs synthesis. Lyophilizer (purchased from Shanghai Yuming Instrument Co., Ltd., model: FD-1A-50) was used for the freeze-drying of CDs, CSCA, and CCNPs products at −60 °C. 3D bioprinter (Inkresible+, Cellink, USA) were used to print NACA and CCNACA scaffolds. Fluorescence photometer (Hitachi F7000, Japan) was used to detect the fluorescence spectra of CDs, CCNPs, NACA and CCNACA. Ultraviolet-visible spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer Company, Lambda 750S) was used to detect the absorption spectra of CDs, CCNPs. Fourier transform near-infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Brooke, Germany, model: MPA) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (thermo, ESCALAB XI+) was used to analyze the structure and functional groups of CDs, CCNPs, CSCA, NACA and CCNACA. Transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL Japan Electronics Co., Ltd., JEM-F200) was used for the morphology and particle size analysis of CDs and CCNPs. DLS analyzer (Malvern Instruments Co., Ltd., UK, model: ZS90) was used for size analysis of CDs and CCNPs. Rheometer (Anton Paar MCR302, Austria) was used for modulus analysis of NACA and CCNACA inks. Contact goniometer (KSV Instruments, Monroe, CT, USA) equipped with a CCD camera (KGV-5000, USA75) was used to analyze the water absorption properties of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds. Two-photon laser confocal (Olympus, FV1000) was used for CDs endocytosis assay, biocompatibility test of NACA and CCNACA and detection of cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS). Microplate reader (Thermo Fisher Scientific Co., Varioskan Flash) was used for CCK8 assay and DPPH and ABTS assay. Inverted fluorescence microscope (Olympus, IX83) was used for drug release detection at the cellular level and cytotoxicity detection of CCNPs. SEM (Japan Electronics Corporation, model: JSM-7610F Plus) was used to detect the morphology of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds.

Dissolve citric acid (CA) and p-phenylenediamine (PP) (CA: PP molar ratio is 3: 1) in 10 mL formamide. After ultrasonic treatment for 10 min, transfer the CA/PP mixed solution to a round bottom flask, put the mixed solution into an oil bath, heat at 180 °C for 1 h, and after reaction, the products cooled to room temperature are placed in a centrifuge at 14,000 rpm (centrifuge at 17,248g) for 15 min. The supernatant was loaded into a dialysis bag, deionized water was used to dialysis the supernatant for 48 h, and was freeze dried to obtain dark brown powder (CDs).

The prepared CDs (5 mg) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and stirred well for use. Cur (1 mg) was dissolved in 3 mL of DMF, stirred well, the Cur solution was slowly added to the CDs aqueous solution (10 mL) and stirred overnight. The reacted solution was centrifuge (centrifuge at 1078g) for 5 min, remove unreacted precipitates (Cur), the supernatant in a dialysis bag (Mw: 3.5 kDa), and dialysis with deionized water for 24 h. The prepared nanoparticles CCNPs were freeze-dried to form yellow powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of caffeic acid grafted chitosan (CSCA)
ce:section.ce:para: Add 1 g of chitosan to a 2 % acetic acid solution (50 mL), stir to form a chitosan solution. Caffeic acid, EDC, NHS, MES, were added to 60 % ethanol solution (50 mL) and mixed. The mixture was slowly added to the chitosan solution, and the pH of the solution was adjusted to 5.0. Let the reaction sit in dark for 10 h, the reacted solution was placed in a dialysis bag (Mw: 14 KDa) and dialyzed in acidified secondary water (pH = 5.5) for 24 h, followed by 4 h of dialysis in ultrapure water, and finally freeze-dried to obtain Caffeic Acid grafted Chitosan (CSCA).

Chitosan and CSCA were dissolved in D2O and caffeic acid was dissolved in DMSO‑d 6 (TMS). 1H NMR spectra was recorded at room temperature using a Bruker AVANCE III 500 NMR system (Switzerland) at 500 MHz.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and performance tests of NACA/CCNACA inks
ce:section.ce:para: We carried out the preparation of two printing inks, one was NACA ink, which was a mixture of nanoclay (NA), CSCA and sodium alginate (Alg). The other was CCNACA ink, which was made from CCNPs, NA, CSCA and Alg. For the preparation of NACA ink, Alg (2 % by mass), NA (9 % by mass) and CSCA (3 % by mass) dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water was stirred with magnetic stirring to obtain NACA ink. For the preparation of CCNACA ink, similarly to the NACA ink, Alg (mass fraction of 2 %), NA (mass fraction of 9 %) and CSCA (3 % by mass), CCNPs containing 4 mg Cur were added, then dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water, and stirred to obtain CCNACA ink.

A rheometer was used to examine the physical characteristics of NACA and CCNACA inks. We performed stable viscosity tests on NACA and CCNACA inks at shear rates ranging from 0.01 to 1000 s−1. Storage modulus (G′) and loss modulus (G″) as well as viscosity characteristics were tested by temperature-sensitive testing of NACA and CCNACA inks at temperatures ranging from 40 °C to 10 °C, respectively, with a cooling rate of 1 °C/min and an oscillation frequency of 1 Hz. It is worth noting that the test mode is the standard vibration mode, and the motion direction of the rotor is two-way rotational vibration. Freshly prepared and degassing samples were maintained at that specific temperature for 2 min without preshear or oscillation prior to performing all rheological tests at each temperature. To prevent dehydration during rheological measurements, a thin layer of low-viscosity paraffin oil was applied to the exposed surface of the hydrogel. Amplitude tests were performed to determine the viscosity and elastic properties of NACA and CCNPs inks at a constant temperature of 20 °C and a constant strain range of 1 %. In addition, the thixotropic characteristics of the printing ink can also affect its printable state. Therefore, we performed thixotropic tests on NACA inks and CCNACA inks in a standard steady state, that is, a continuous and stable shear mode, and the rotor rotates counterclockwise in the same direction. During the test, the shear rate ranges from 0.01 to 1000 s−1 and the temperature was set at 20 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and physical characteristics of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: Add NACA ink and CCNACA ink into the polyethylene injection cartridge, separately, and select 260 μm as the printing needle. Load the programmed CAD model file (with a grid structure of 8 × 8 × 2 mm) into the computer 3D printing software (Slic3r). Next, the printing parameters were adjusted to a pressure of 180 ± 5 kPa, a printing speed of 3 mm s−1, and a distance of 1 mm between the operating table and the printing needle. After everything was ready, the NACA ink and the CCNACA ink were printed with a 3D bioprinter at 25 °C, separately, to form a NACA composite scaffold and a CCNACA composite scaffold. After printing, scaffolds were immersed in a Ca2+ aqueous solution for 1 h at room temperature for ion crosslinking. After crosslinking, the NACA composite scaffolds and CCNACA composite scaffolds were freeze-dried. The two hybrid scaffolds (NACA composite scaffold and CCNACA composite scaffold) were then immersed in EDC, NHS, MES for secondary cross-linking for 6 h. Once cross-linking completed, the scaffolds (NACA composite scaffolds and CCNACA composite scaffolds) were treated by washing with alcohol (Valcohol: VH2O = 60 %) for 1 h, and then ultrapream water for 3 times (30 min each time) to wash off the cross-linking agent. Finally, the 3D printed NACA composite scaffolds and CCNACA composite scaffolds were obtained by freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical property test of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: According to the preparation methods of the two hybrid scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA scaffolds) described in Section 2.10, the lyophilized composite scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA scaffolds) were put into phosphate buffer solution (PBS) to mimic the state of the composite scaffolds under physiological conditions. The compression modulus of the cross-linked 3D printed composite scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA scaffolds) were measured by universal experimental machine. It is worth mentioning that the surface height of the sample was guaranteed to be neat. During the compression test, the loading speed of longitudinal compression of the scaffold material was 1 mm/min, and the compression volume was 60 %. The formula E = σ/ε = yl/ab (1-x) for the compression modulus of the 3D printed composite scaffold (NACA/CCNACA scaffold). Where, E is the compression modulus (unit: MPa); σ is the stress per unit area, σ = F/A = y/ab; ε is strain, that is, the ratio of the deformation of the support to the total length, ε = (l-x)/l; Where y is the pressure load (unit: N); a, b, l are the length, width and height of the support respectively (unit: mm); X is the displacement of the support under pressure load (unit: mm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water absorption test of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para:
Take the freeze-dried 3D printing scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA) to weigh the dry weight of the sample, expressed in m1. Place it in a 24 well plate and soak it in PBS solution at room temperature for 2 h, 4 h, 6 h, 8 h, 10 h, 12 h and 24 h, respectively. Take out the scaffolds, wipe the water on the surface of the scaffolds with absorbent paper, and weigh and record its weight as m2.
(1)
Water absorption of the scaffolds : φ
%
=
m 2
−
m 1

m 1
× 100

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle tests of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: The contact angles of the lyophilized composite scaffold (NACA/CCNACA composite scaffold) were measured with a contact goniometer equipped with a CCD camera. Simply, drop 6 μL of deionized water on the surface of the scaffold, take pictures at the same time, and calculate the contact angle by analyzing the collected images.

For the optical performance test of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds, we also used fluorescence photometer to test the fluorescence spectrum. Different from the solution state, the NACA/CCNACA scaffolds were immersed in PBS to simulate the state in the physiological environment. The above two scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA scaffolds) were fixed on the solid fluorescence operating table, and then placed in the fluorescence photometer to test the fluorescence spectrum.

NIH 3 T3 (3T3, mouse embryonic fibroblasts, purchased from Wuhan Pnuosai Life Technology Co., LTD.) and MCF-7 (Human breast cancer cells, Cell lines purchased from Wuhan Pnuosai Life Technology Co., LTD.) were cultured in medium (DMEM, GIBCO, USA) containing 10 % (V: V) heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS, Hangzhou Sijiqing Engineering Materials Co., LTD.) and 1 % (V: V) double antibody (Wuhan Pnuosai Life Technology Co., LTD.). Cells were cultured at 37 °C in a humidified incubator (Thermo fisher scientific, HERAcell 150, USA) with 5 % CO2, and the medium was changed once a day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of reactive oxygen species (ROS) in cells
ce:section.ce:para: Before the biological experiment, it is the key to ensure the asepsis of the material, so we first do a good job of material sterilization. CDs and Cur were sterilized by high pressure at 120 °C because of their good thermal stability. CCNPs was sterilized by filter sterilization. The prepared CCNPs solution was filtered in the 0.22 μm sterilization filter, then the filtered CCNPs solution was placed in the ultraviolet environment and sterilized for 24 h, and finally used in the biological experiment. The 3D printing environment of the scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA) is aseptic, and the whole printing process is completed in the ultraviolet sterilization environment. Before the biological experiment, the CCNACA scaffolds were sterilized in ultraviolet environment for 24 h.

3 T3 and MCF-7 cells were seeded in 48-well plates at an initial density of 5 × 103 cells/well. The inoculated cells were incubated in a 37 °C incubator for 24 h, and then the cell culture medium was removed. Drugs (CDs with 50 μg/mL, Cur with 50 μg/mL, CCNPs with 50 μg/mL, CS with 10 mg/mL, CA with 10 mg/mL, CSCA with 10 mg/mL, NACS with 10 mg/mL and CCNACA with 10 mg/mL) dissolved in serum-free basal medium were added to treat the cells, and the cells were incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in the dark. Note that we use serum free culture solution to dilute the positive control H2O2 (100 μM working solution concentration), add it to the well plate of the inoculated cells, and incubate it in a 37 °C cell incubator for 30 min in the dark. Next, the drug was removed, cells were washed twice with serum-free medium, and DCFH-DA was diluted with serum-free medium to a final concentration of 10 μM. 200 μL diluted DCFH-DA working solution was added and incubated in a 37 °C cell incubator for 30 min in the dark. Finally, fluorescence imaging was observed under a two-photon laser confocal microscope.

Briefly, the printed scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA) were placed in 75 % alcohol and stirred for 24 h, then placed in a clean bench for UV sterilization overnight, and then soaked in PBS for 3 h to remove the residual ethanol solution. Next, the scaffolds were placed in 24-well plates and blown dry to a semi-dry state with a fan, and 3 T3 and MCF-7 cells were seeded on bactericidal treated printed scaffolds at an initial density of 2 × 104 cells/well and cultured in a 37 °C cell incubator for 24 h. Then cells cultured on the scaffold were added with 20 μL of CCK8 basal medium solution (CCK: basal medium = 1: 10 (v/v)) at 1, 3, 7 and 14 days, respectively, and the well plates were incubated at 37 °C for another 2 h. Finally, the plates were shaken three times and the absorbance was measured at 450 nm with a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calcein-AM/PI staining tests of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: Similar to the cell seeding steps in the CCK8 test in 2.10.1, but the cell density was adjusted to 3 × 104 cells/well and seeded on the treated printed scaffolds. The medium was removed at 1, 3, 7 and 14 days, and added AM/PI in PBS (AM: PI: PBS = 2: 1: 1000 (v/v/v), 100 μL), then place the cells with the dye (AM/PI) into the cell incubator to avoid The light was incubated for 40 min, followed by live/dead cell imaging detection by an inverted fluorescence microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scanning electron microscope (SEM) inspection of NACA/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: The seeding process and number of cells were exactly the same as in 2.15.2. On the 1, 3, 7 and 14 days after cells were inoculated into the scaffolds, the medium in the pore plate was sucked out, and 2.5 % (Glutaraldehyde: PBS (v: v)) glutaraldehyde was used to fix in the environment of 4 °C for 3 h. Then ethanol solutions of different concentrations (50 %, 70 %, 90 % and 100 %) were added in turn for progressive dehydration. Each dehydration time was 30 min. After dehydration, they were put into room temperature for drying. Finally, the morphology of cells on the scaffolds were observed under a SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of drug release curve of CCNPs/CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para:
For drug release from CCNPs, we first prepared different pH buffer solutions. Using citric acid monohydrate and NaH2PO4 as raw materials to prepare a pH = 5.0 buffer. NaH2PO4·2H2O and Na2HPO4·12H2O as raw materials to prepare a pH = 6.5 buffer. And NaCl, KCl, KH2PO4 and Na2HPO4·12H2O were used to prepare buffer with pH = 7.4. Then, CCNPs were dissolved in phosphate buffer (3 mL) at different pH values (pH = 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4) and then placed in dialysis bags (Mw: 3500 kDa). Dialysis bags were placed in tubes containing buffer solutions (17 mL) at different pH (pH = 5.0, 6.5 and pH = 7.4) and incubated at 37 °C. Over a predetermined period of time (1, 3, 7, 14 and 28 days), 1 mL of buffer solution outside the dialysis bag was removed and replaced with 1 mL of fresh buffer solution. The OD of Cur was measured at 432 nm with a microplate reader, and then the release of Cur in the same pH buffer solution was calculated according to the standard curve of Cur. Finally, the relative percentage of the cumulative mass released to the total drug was calculated as a function of the different incubation time points. For drug release of CCNACA scaffolds, CCNACA scaffolds was placed in phosphate buffer solution (20 mL) with different pH (pH = 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4) and incubated at 37 °C. Next, within a predetermined period of time (1, 3, 7, 14 and 28 days), 1 mL of buffer solution is taken out and replaced with 1 mL of fresh buffer solution. Use the microplate reader to measure the OD value of Cur at 432 nm, and then calculate the release amount of Cur in the same pH buffer solution according to the standard curve of Cur. Finally, the relative percentage of the cumulative mass released to the total drug was calculated as a function of the different incubation times.
(2)
Drug release ratio
%
=
m release
/
M total
× 100 %
mrelease: the mass of the drug cumulatively released;
Mtotal: the total mass of the CCNPs/CCNACA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release of CCNACA scaffolds in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: 3 T3 and MCF-7 cells were seeded in 6-well plates at an initial density of 3 × 103 cells/well, and the plates inoculated with cells were incubated for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C. The prepared CCNACA was placed into the well plate containing cells. The scaffolds were removed at 1, 3, 7 and 14 days, respectively, and the medium was aspirated and added into PBS solution with AM/PI (AM: PI: PBS = 2: 1: 1000 (v/v/v), 100 μL) and then incubated in a cell incubator in darkness for 40 min, followed by live/dead cell imaging under an inverted fluorescence microscope.

Phosphate buffered solutions (PBS) with pH of 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4 were selected to simulate the physiological environment and tumor microenvironment respectively (all samples in triplicate). The dried scaffolds (NACA/CCNACA scaffolds) were weighed and labeled as W0, and then put into PBS solutions with different pH (5.0, 6.5 and 7.4). After a specific time (1, 3, 7 and 14 days), the hydrogels were taken out, and then the scaffolds were washed three times with secondary water to remove excess PBS. Finally, the scaffolds were lyophilized and weighed, Mark the quality as Wt.
