ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of animal and tumor model
ce:section.ce:para: Female Balb/c nude mice (4–6 weeks old, weighing about 15 g) were provided by SPF Animal Experimental Center of Dalian Medical University (Dalian, China). Throughout the experiment, all mice were kept under the required conditions and had free access to food and sterile water. MCF-7 breast cancer cells (8 × 106) were inoculated into the left limb (subcutaneous) of Balb/c nude mice to establish a mouse breast cancer model (2 × 2 × 2 mm). All operations on Balb/c nude mice were performed in accordance with relevant laws and in accordance with the requirements of the Chinese Medical Ethics Committee, and in accordance with the Biological and Medical Ethics Committee of Dalian University of Technology (2021-065).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo wound healing and anti-tumor effect of CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the wound healing and anti-tumor effect of CCNACA scaffolds in vivo, a mouse breast cancer model was established. In short, after fasting for 24 h, Balb/c nude mice were injected with 20 μL/g Avertin (anesthetic). After the mice were in a coma, the digested MCF-7 breast cancer cells (8 × 106/200 μL) were inoculated into the left limb (subcutaneously) of Balb/c nude mice. During the experiment, all mice were given normal food and water, and the status of mice was observed in real time. 5 days later, MCF-7 breast cancer cells were inoculated to form a breast cancer model. The next step was to remove the tumor. To put it simply, after the mice were anesthetized, they were fixed on the operating table for tumor resection. After the tumor was removed, NACA and CCNACA scaffolds were implanted into the surgical defects of the mice. It is worth mentioning that the mice without stent implantation served as the blank control group. Then, the wound healing and anti-tumor effects of Balb/c nude mice were observed on the 3rd, 7th and 14th day after scaffolds treatment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug release of CCNACA scaffolds in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: In order to study the drug release of CCNACA scaffolds in mice, fluorescence imaging and FL semi-quantitative analysis were performed by Maestro EX small animal fluorescence imaging system on the 3rd, 7th and 14th day after CCNACA/NACA scaffolds implantation. In addition, the mice without stents were used as blank control.

Heart, liver, spleen, lung, kidney and other organs, as well as skin tissue samples treated at different times (3, 7 and 14 days, respectively). The above samples were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde, embedded in paraffin, and then stained with Hematoxylin-Eosin (HE) and Masson. After that, the slice was fixed on the slide and observed under an optical microscope.

Origin 8 and GraphPad Prism software were used to analyze the data of each group, and the data (n = 3) were expressed as mean and standard deviation. Data between groups were analyzed by one-way analysis of variance.

Using citric acid and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials and formamide as solvent, we synthesized carbon dots (CDs) with good water solubility and biocompatibility as fluorescent imaging diagnostic reagents through pyrolysis. In order to explore the optimal emission wavelength of the prepared CDs, we studied the optical properties of the CDs through fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy tests. Fig. 1a showed the absorption spectra of CDs aqueous solution at different concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200, 400 μg/mL). The data showed that CDs had three absorption peaks at 240 nm, 350 nm and 460 nm, respectively. They were attributed to the π-π* transition (240 nm) of the CC bond, the n-π * transition (350 nm) of the CO bond, and the n-π* transition (460 nm) of the extended conjugate system of CO and CN, respectively (Cao et al., 2022; Su et al., 2020a, b). Importantly, the absorption intensities of CDs increased significantly with increasing concentrations, but the position of absorption peak was not affected by concentration. Next, we analyzed the fluorescence spectrum of CDs according to the absorption spectrum. Fig. 1b–f showed the fluorescence spectrum of CDs at different concentrations (25, 50, 100, 200 and 400 μg/mL, respectively). The data showed that for each specific concentration, the emission wavelength of CDs was generally redshifted with the increase of excitation wavelength, which confirmed that CDs had excitation dependent luminescence behavior. In addition, each concentration had the strongest emission fluorescence at the same wavelength, indicating that CDs did not exhibit concentration dependent luminescence behavior (Zhi et al., 2020). According to the fluorescence image of CDs, the maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength were respectively taken as functions of different concentrations (Fig. 2a), and the fluorescence spectrogram was quantitatively analyzed more intuitively. In conclusion, the fluorescence spectrum of CDs was consistent with the absorption spectrum phenomenon, and maintained excellent luminescence performance (Su, Xie, & Zheng, 2020).

Firstly, CDs was used as a carrier, dissolved in deionized water and stirred evenly. Cur was then dissolved in DMF and stirred until completely dissolved, and then stirred for 12 h to allow the CDs and Cur to fully assemble. The collected mixture was then at 3500 rpm (centrifuge at 1078g) for 5 min to remove unassembled Cur. CDs-Cur nanoparticles (CCNPs) were obtained after freeze-drying. The loading ratio of CCNPs was obtained from the standard curve of Cur (Fig. S1a), and the calculated load ratio of Cur in CCNPs was 40.7 %. In order to verify the successful assembly of CDs and Cur, the luminous characteristics and structural characteristics were analyzed respectively. First, we tested the luminescence of Cur. Considering that Cur is insoluble in water, and the fluorescence spectrum cannot be measured, we therefore tested its fluorescence luminescence in DMF solution. As shown in Fig. 2b, Cur showed the excitation independent fluorescence characteristics of emission wavelength, and the fluorescence emission intensity was the largest at 500 nm excitation wavelength. For the luminescence test of CCNPs, as shown in Fig. 2c, the emission intensity of CCNPs depended on the excitation wavelength in the same way as CDs (Fig. 1b–f), but also retained the non-excitation dependent luminescence characteristic of Cur itself. Moreover, under the same concentration of Cur, the fluorescence intensity of CCNPs was significantly enhanced compared with Cur solution. The above test results showed that Cur had been successfully loaded on CDs. We verified the fluorescence spectrum test results by absorption spectra. As shown in Fig. 2d, the absorption peaks of CDs were displayed at 240 nm, 350 nm and 460 nm positions, respectively, and the absorption peaks had a large range. However, for CCNPs, there was only one absorption peak at 432 nm, which was the same as that of Cur, but the absorption intensity was enhanced. The above absorption spectrum data were exactly consistent with the fluorescence emission characteristics of CCNPs relative to CDs and Cur (Wen et al., 2022). The successful assembly of CDs and Cur was further demonstrated (Su et al., 2020a, b).

Next, the structures of CDs, Cur and CCNPs were all analyzed. Firstly, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was used to analyze the functional group characteristics of CDs, Cur and CCNPs. As shown in Fig. 2e, for CCNPs, stretching vibration spectra of NH and -OH bonds at 3319 cm−1 were displayed. Then, the vibrational absorption band of CO in the carboxyl group was located in the absorption band at 1688 cm−1, and the above functional groups were consistent with CDs (Atchudan et al., 2018; Su et al., 2020a, b; Ye et al., 2020). In addition, the bands of CCNPs at 1277 cm−1 and 1154 cm−1 were attributed to the aromatic CO stretching and C-O-C stretching modes, respectively, which corresponded to the bands of Cur FTIR (Banerjee, 2014; Rezaei & Nasirpour, 2018; Tang, Wang, Pan, Weng, & Huang, 2007). In summary, CCNPs combined the functional groups of both CDs and Cur materials, indicating that CDs successfully loaded with Cur.

In the full-scan XPS spectrum, as shown in Fig. 2f, the XPS of CDs sample presented three peaks, which could be attributed to O1s (532 eV), N1s (400 eV) and C1s (285 eV), and the corresponding contents of each element were 39.4 %, 24.6 % and 36.0 %, respectively. Cur sample showed only two peaks, O1s (532 eV, 44.2 %) and C1s (285 eV, 55.8 %). For CCNPs, there were three peaks at 532 ev, 400ev and 285ev, corresponding to O1s (42.0), N1s (9.7 %) and C1s (48.3 %), respectively (Atchudan et al., 2017; Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2016). It indicated that the functional groups of CDs and Cur were successfully combined, so that CCNPs had not only C and O elements, but also N elements of CDs. Then we carried out high-resolution XPS spectra to further analyze the relationship between the functional groups of CDs, Cur and CCNPs. Fig. S2a–c showed the high-resolution XPS spectra of CDs, Fig. S2d–f represented the high-resolution XPS spectra of Cur, and Fig. S2g–i represented the high-resolution XPS spectra of CCNPs. According to the data, the high-resolution spectra of O 1s of CDs, Cur and CCNPs all showed two peaks, corresponding to C-O(285.8 eV) and CO (288.4 eV), respectively. In addition, for C1s high-resolution spectrum, CDs, Cur and CCNPs all had the same functional groups, namely C-C/C=C (284.7ev), C-O/C-N (286.1 ev) and CO (288.9 ev). It was worth mentioning that Cur had no N element, but the N1s (NH, 401.0 ev) binding energy was contained in the N1s high-resolution XPS spectrum of CCNPs (Moradi, Zinadini, Rajabi, & Ashraf Derakhshan, 2020), which was the same as the N1s binding energy of CDs. The above results further proved that CCNPs was successfully assembled by CDs and Cur.

The morphology and particle size of CDs were analyzed under transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 3a, the prepared CDs showed good monodispersity with an average diameter of 5.61 ± 0.36 nm (inset: particle size distribution). Next, TEM tests were performed on CCNPs. As shown in Fig. 3b (Fig. 3c was a local magnification of Fig. 3b), CCNPs were spheroidal and had an average particle size of 118.24 ± 0.83 nm (inset: particle size distribution), showing a larger size than CDs. The average size of CCNPs was further verified by dynamic light scattering (DLS) test (Fig. 3d). The average size of CCNPs was 137.90 ± 0.52 nm, and the PDI was 0.18, indicating that CCNPs had good dispersion, which was basically consistent with TEM data (Su et al., 2020a, b; Su, Xie, & Zheng, 2020). Physical stability is a key factor to satisfy medical applications. Therefore, we investigated the particle size stability of CCNPs. As shown in Fig. 3e, within two weeks of dispersion in aqueous solution, the size of CCNPs ranged from 120 nm to 130 nm, and their size changes were negligible. Moreover, the PDI value at different times always remained below 0.2, indicating that the dispersion of CCNPs was stable and no aggregation occurred. Considering the in vivo blood environment, we further evaluated the particle size stability of CCNPs in 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS) using the same procedure. As shown in Fig. 3f, the particle size of CCNPs was stable, and the dispersion of CCNPs did not change. The above results showed that CCNPs had nanostructure and good physical stability, which could lay a foundation for biomedical applications (Su et al., 2020a, b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake of CCNPs
ce:section.ce:para: As we all know, the in vitro cell experiments are the premise of biomedical applications, so we first explored the cell uptake of CCNPs to determine whether they can be internalized into cells to play a role. We observed the uptake of CCNPs in cells directly under two-photon laser confocal scanning microscopy. Fig. 4a–c represented the uptake of CDs, Cur and CCNPs to MCF-7 cells. As shown in Fig. 4a, MCF-7 cells incubated with CDs for 6 h showed a multi-colour fluorescence luminescence characteristic, and with the increase of the concentration of CDs, the fluorescence intensity gradually increased, indicating that CDs could be absorbed by cells to play a good fluorescence imaging function. However, no obvious fluorescence signal was detected in Cur treated MCF-7 cells (Fig. 4b). Even if the concentration increased, only very weak and negligible fluorescence could be observed, mainly because the water solubility of Cur molecule was poor, and molecular aggregation was therefore easy to occur in cell culture medium, leading to precipitation, which prevented Cur from internalizing into cells. For the CCNPs experimental group (Fig. 4c), after the MCF-7 cells were incubated with CCNPs for 6 h, the fluorescence appeared in the MCF-7 cells treated with different concentrations of CCNPs, and the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the increasing concentrations. The above luminescence phenomenon was consistent with the luminescence of CDs, indicating that Cur successfully combined with CDs, which not only combined the luminescence characteristics of CDs, but also solved the problem of water solubility of Cur. Next, we used Image J software to further quantitatively measure the uptake efficiency of CCNPs by cells (Wu et al., 2020).

As shown in Fig. 4d–f, the cells treated with CDs and CCNPs did show polychrome luminescence, and the fluorescence intensity increased gradually with the increase of concentrations. For Cur group as shown in Fig. 4e, cells could hardly display fluorescence, that is, they were not effectively internalized by MCF-7 cells. The above phenomena were basically consistent with the results of cell endocytosis experiment. To sum up, CCNPs can be effectively internalized by MCF-7 cells and have a good cellular imaging effect, which also lays a favorable foundation for later tumor treatment (Ma et al., 2019).

Having established that CCNPs could be endocy4tosed by MCF-7 cells, the cytotoxicity of CCNPs was subsequently determined. First, we performed in vitro toxicity assays of CDs, Cur and CCNPs at different concentrations (3, 6, 12, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200 and 300 μg/mL, respectively) using CCK-8 assay (He et al., 2021; Sun et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 4g, even when CDs reached the maximum concentration of 300 μg/mL, the cell survival ratio of MCF-7 cells and 3 T3 cells were still >97 %, indicating that CDs had no obvious cytotoxicity, no matter against breast cancer cells or fibroblast cells. For Cur-treated cells (MCF-7 cells and 3 T3 cells), as shown in Fig. 5a, under different concentrations of Cur, the survival ratio of 3 T3 cells was always above 96 %, while for Cur-treated MCF-7 cells, with the increase of concentrations, at the concentrations of 200 and 300 μg/mL Cur, the cell survival ratio of MCF-7 were 87.82 ± 2.68 % and 81.01 ± 3.12 %, respectively, which only had slight cytotoxicity, mainly due to the poor water solubility of Cur and the inability to endocytose cells for therapeutic effect. For CCNPs-treated cells, as shown in Fig. 5b, the survival ratio of cells treated with CCNPs at different concentrations (0, 3, 6, 12, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, and 300 μg/mL) were 99.62 ± 1.13 % and 90.51 ± 3.43 %, 84.52 ± 2.65 %, 68.72 ± 1.38 %, 63.92 ± 4.88 %, 61.92 ± 2.77 %, 52.03 ± 2.45 %, 32.23 ± 1.38 %, 24.51 ± 2.85 % and 9.83 ± 0.78 %, respectively. The results showed that CCNPs had no obvious toxicity to MCF-7 cells at a lower concentration, but the toxicity increased significantly with the increase of the concentration, and the cell survival ratio was only 9.83 % in the condition of the maximum concentration (300 μg/mL). Following this, we investigated the cytotoxic effect of CCNPs on 3 T3 cells. As shown in Fig. 5b, the cell survival ratio of 3 T3 cells treated with CCNPs at the maximum concentration (300 μg/mL) still reached 71.01 ± 3.18 %, indicating that CCNPs had no obvious cytotoxicity to fibroblast cells (3 T3) and would not affect their normal growth and function.

In addition, in order to visually study the cytotoxicity of CCNPs on cells, we used live/dead staining assay to verify cell necrosis under drug treatment. Cells (MCF-7, 3 T3) were incubated with 300 μg/mL CCNPs and stained with the fluorescent probes calcein AM (AM, 5 μg/mL) and propidium iodide (PI, 50 μg/mL) synchronously (Kopp et al., 2020). Fig. 5c–e demonstrated the toxic effects of CDs, Cur and CCNPs on MCF-7 cells respectively. The results showed that the cells treated with CDs and Cur emitted bright green fluorescence. Although Cur group had a small amount of red fluorescence, it did not affect the normal growth of MCF-7 cells, indicating that neither CDs nor Cur had obvious cytotoxicity. While the number of dead cells in CCNPs treated cells increased significantly and showed strong red fluorescence in Fig. 5e, indicating that CCNPs had obvious cytotoxicity to MCF-7, which was consistent with the data of CCK-8. In addition, the toxic effects of CDs, Cur and CCNPs on fibroblast cells (3 T3 cells), as shown in Fig. 5f–h, all groups showed strong green fluorescence. Although a small amount of red fluorescence appeared in 3 T3 cells treated with CCNPs (concentration of 300 μg/mL), it did not affect the normal growth of 3 T3 cells. Therefore, it can be concluded that CDs, Cur and CCNPs have no obvious cytotoxicity to fibroblast cells (3 T3 cells). This phenomenon was also corresponding to CCK-8 test results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and rheological properties of CCNACA ink
ce:section.ce:para: The above contents have verified the excellent tumor killing effect of CCNPs. However, due to the small size of nanoparticles, they are easy to be quickly cleared in the blood circulation in the process of disease diagnosis and treatment, and thus cannot effectively reach the lesion site, resulting in low drug utilization ratio. Therefore, in order to better solve the problems of wound repair and prognosis recurrence after local resection of early breast cancer, we added the prepared CCNPs to a 3D printed scaffold to form a local drug release system, which is a topic worth studying.

Caffeic acid (CA) is a phytochemical substance rich in phenolic hydroxyl, which has extensive antibacterial, antioxidant and anti-virus activities. In this study, he carboxyl group on CA was grafted onto the amino group of chitosan (CS) using EDC/NHS/MES cross-linking agent to obtain CA grafted CS (CSCA). The obtained CSCA solved the problem of water solubility of CS on the one hand, and increased the antibacterial and antioxidant properties of CS on the other hand. First of all, we use Folin phenol to calculate that the content of total phenol in CSCA is 30.3 %, which proves that CA is successfully grafted onto CS (Fig. S1b, auxiliary information) (Kopp et al., 2020; Degirmenci & Erkurt, 2020). Then we verify the successful preparation of CSCA by 1H NMR. Fig. S1c–e shows the 1H NMR spectra of CA, CSCA and CS, respectively. CS shows a single peak at 1.97 ppm, representing the methyl of the N-acetylglucosamine unit. The peak positions of 2.98 ppm, 3.60–3.97 ppm and 4.15 ppm correspond to H2, H3-H6 and H1 on CS, respectively. Compared with chitosan, CSCA has a new peak at 6.18–7.41 ppm, which is attributed to the methylene proton of CA. The above data once again confirmed the successful grafting of caffeic acid onto chitosan (İlyasoğlu, Nadzieja, & Guo, 2019; Wang et al., 2019). We determined the degree of chemical coupling of caffeic acid using 1H NMR spectroscopy and calculated that the degree of catechol substitution was 12.71 per 100 glucose rings of chitosan (Lee et al., 2020). Next, we further verified the grafting of CS and CA by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR). As shown in Fig. S3a, the FTIR spectrum of CS showed peaks at 3540 cm−1 (OH extension) and 1638 cm−1 (amide II vibration band, NH bending). For CA alone, the OH tension on the benzene ring occurred at 3477 cm−1. In addition, the CO vibration peak of the carboxyl group (1618 cm−1) could also be found. Compared with the above separate spectral data of CS and CA, we found that on the one hand, the FTIR spectra of CSCA retained some functional groups of CS and CA, such as the OH extension of CS surface (3531 cm−1); On the other hand, a new peak appeared, for example, the amide bond I, CO tensile vibration appeared at 1643 cm−1, indicating that the carboxyl group of CA was successfully coupled with the amino group of CS. In addition, there was an aromatic CC peak in the spectrum of CSCA, which was located at 1551 cm−1 and belonged to the catechol group of CSCA (İlyasoğlu, Nadzieja, & Guo, 2019; Huang et al., 2022).

Next, we further evaluated the structure of CSCA by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. S3b showed the XPS full scan spectra of CS, CA and CSCA. The results confirmed that CS has three main peaks: C1s (286.6 ev), O 1s (531.0 ev) and N 1s (397.0 ev). CA had two main peaks: C1s (282.3 ev) and O 1s (535.2 ev). In addition to C1s (286.9 ev) and O 1s (535.6 ev), CSCA also owned N elements at 402.0 ev. Next, we analyzed the functional groups of the material by the XPS spectra of CS, CA and CSCA, respectively (Huang et al., 2022; Souza Campelo, Chevallier, Loy, Silveira Vieira, & Mantovani, 2020). Fig. S3c–e showed the high-resolution spectra of N 1s (CN and NH functional groups), O 1s (CO and CO functional groups), and C 1s (CN, C-O/C-N-C=O and N-C=O functional groups) of CS, respectively. Fig. S3f–h displayed the high-resolution spectra of O1s (CO and CO functional groups) and C 1s (CC, CO and CO functional groups) of CA, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of each element of CSCA were shown in Fig. S3i–k. The high-resolution spectra of N1S (Fig. S3i) were deconvolved into two peaks at 399.7 ev (CN) and 401.8 ev (NH), and the peak area at 401.8 ev was significantly higher than that in CS. A possible explanation for this phenomenon is that the -COOH of CA is attached to the -NH2 of CS, thereby causing a change in the energy state of the N element. For the O1s band (Fig. S3j), two peaks could be fitted at 531.8 ev (CO) and 532.9 ev (C=O). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. S3k) could be divided into three peaks: C-N/C=N (285.0 ev), C-O/C-N (286.8 ev) and C=O/N-N=O (288.7 ev). Similarly, this is mainly due to the introduction of CA. Based on the above results, the carboxyl group of CA combined with the amino group of CS to form acylamine.

After preparing the functional materials, the ink was prepared. We mixed sodium alginate (Alg), nano clay (NC) and CSCA with nano drugs (CCNPs) to form CCNACA ink. As a control, we prepared ink (NACA) with Alg, NC and CSCA as raw materials. It is well known that the rheological behavior of hydrogels is a prerequisite for evaluating 3D printing. Next, we tested various rheological properties of the ink.

First, we need to study the printable temperature of the ink. As shown in Fig. 6a, the viscosity of both NACA and CCNACA inks decreases with the increase of temperature, indicating that the prepared ink has good temperature sensitivity, and the addition of nano drugs (CCNPs) has no effect on the temperature sensitivity of the ink. Next, we study the effect of temperature on G′ and G″ (Heo et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 6b, we measured the variation of ink G′ and G″ with temperature (in the range of 10–40 °C) with a rheometer at a cooling rate of 1 °C/min and an oscillation frequency of 1 Hz. The data show that at the initial temperature of low temperature, the G′ of both NACA and CCNACA ink is significantly higher than that of G″. With the increase of temperature, the gap between G′ and G″ gradually decreases, but G' is always higher than that of G″. This indicates that the prepared ink (NACA/CCNACA) can maintain the shape of the printed scaffold while having temperature sensitivity. Next, G′ and G″ were measured for different angular frequency variations (Liu et al., 2019). As shown in Fig. 6c, G′ and G″ of both NACA and CCNACA inks did not change significantly at low and high frequencies, and the average G′ at the study frequency was always higher than G″, indicating that the elastic properties of NACA and CCNACA were better than the viscous properties and had good printability (Kim et al., 2018). It further indicates that the structures of NACA or CCNACA have a strong interconnection network, which has relatively stable physical characteristics. To investigate the shear thinning properties of NACA or CCNACA inks, we measured the viscosity as a function of increasing shear rate. It can be seen from Fig. 6d that the viscosity of NACA or CCNACA ink decreases with the increase of shear rate at low shear rate, and the change is significant. At a high shear rate (>100 s−1), although the viscosity of the ink is very low, there is still a phenomenon of viscosity reduction (Fig. 6d inset). The results show that adding CCNPs will not change the shear thinning characteristics of NACA ink (Jessop et al., 2019; Ojansivu, Rashad, et al., 2019). In addition, the thixotropy of the ink represents the time dependence of the fluid viscosity. As shown in Fig. 6e, we measured the shear stress as a function of shear rate, and made the shear stress - Shear rate curve, the results show that there is almost no difference in the shear stress-shear rate function curves of NACA or CCNACA inks, and both of them show a large area, indicating that NACA or CCNACA inks have a large thixotropy, high stability and are not easy to deform (Marapureddy et al., 2021).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and performance characterization of CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: The ink prepared in Section 3.5 was loaded into the printer cartridge, and 0.26 μm printing needle was used for printing. The pressure was adjusted at any time during the printing process to ensure smooth ink extrusion. Then, CaCl2 and EDC/NHS/MES were crosslinked for the printed scaffolds. Finally, a scaffold with good supporting performance and not easy to collapse was formed (Fig. 7a–d). It is worth noting that NC plays an important role in the forming and cross-linking of 3D printing scaffolds. The particle size, potential and structure of NC are shown in Fig. S4. As shown by Fig. S4a, the unit cell of NC consists of two layers of SiO tetrahedron sandwiched with a layer of Mg- (O.OH) trioctahedron, which is dispersed in water to form a transparent thixotropic colloid with an average size of 59.59 ± 0.89 nm. And NC is rich in O−, which makes NC negatively charged. The zeta potential of NC is −31.6 ± 0.52 mV. Due to the strong physical interaction between the polymer chain and the monodisperse NC, the polymer/NC composite can be quickly extruded and maintains its shape, while improving the mechanical properties of the polymer/nano-clay composite and ensuring the formability of the 3D printed scaffolds (Liang et al., 2020). In addition, the SiO group in NC forms hydrogen bonds with the -OH group in the CSCA/Alg polymer, further strengthening the interaction in the CCNACA scaffold system (Siboro et al., 2021; Sheng, Gao, Yin, Kang, & Kong, 2021; X. Li et al., 2021).

Compression tests were performed on the crosslinked 3D printed scaffolds to explore the mechanical properties of NACA or CCNACA scaffolds. Fig. 6f shows that the NACA and CCNACA scaffolds deform and compress when subjected to external compression in the strain range of 0 % to 60 %. At a strain of 0.6, the stresses of the NACA and CCNACA scaffolds were 280.92 kPa and 144.83 kPa, respectively. At the same time, it also shows that the mechanical properties of NACA and CCNACA brackets are similar, the two are in the same order of magnitude, and the mechanical properties of NACA will not be changed by the addition of CCNPs. In addition, in the lower strain range (<40 %), NACA and CCNACA scaffolds showed neither severe plastic deformation nor strength decline, indicating that they have good stability and resilience. As a material for filling and repairing skin, the mechanical properties related to the skin are one of the most important parameters, so we determine the elastic modulus of the material by using the stress-strain slope in the compression test (Su et al., 2022). Results as shown in Fig. S8, the elastic moduli of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds are 49.72 Kpa and 42.51 Kpa, respectively. In order to compare the elastic modulus of real skin tissue, we have conducted a large number of literature studies. Studies have shown that according to the different content of various skin proteins and the different stresses applied to the skin, the elastic modulus of the skin is between 0.02 Mpa–100 Mpa, which is a wide range (Matai, Kaur, Seyedsalehi, McClinton, & Laurencin, 2020; Pailler-Mattei, Bec, & Zahouani, 2008; Zhou et al., 2020). The elastic modulus of this study is between the elastic modulus of the above skin tissue mentioned above. In summary, NACA and CCNACA scaffolds have reliable mechanical strength and can be used as skin substitutes for skin repair.

Next, scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was used to test the micromorphology of the printed scaffolds (NACA and CCNACA) after Ca2+ cross-linking and EDC/NHS/MES cross-linking. From Fig. 7e (Fig. 7f is the local magnification of Fig. 7e), it can be found that the cross-linked CCNA scaffold has obvious pores, irregular and rough surface, laying a foundation for cell adhesion. For CCNACA (Fig. 7g–h), in addition to the rough surface, CCNPs can also be observed (Fig. 7h), indicating that CCNPs have been successfully loaded on the surface of the scaffolds. In addition, 10 areas were randomly selected from the SEM images of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds for energy spectrum analysis. The results in Fig. 7i show that NACA scaffolds are mainly composed of C (27.42 %), O (41.46 %), Mg (11.38 %), Si (17.72 %), N (1.54 %) elements. Fig. 7j shows the element analysis of CCNACA. The results show that NACA scaffolds are mainly composed of C (27.55 %), O (41.91 %), Mg (9.39 %), Si (16.61 %), N (4.54 %) elements. The difference is that the content of C, O and N elements of CCNACA is higher than that of NACA scaffolds, which further proves the successful incorporation of CCNPs.

Next, the luminescence characteristics of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds were studied. Fig. S6a shows the emission spectra of NACA scaffolds at different excitation wavelengths, and the results show that NACA has no fluorescence luminescence phenomenon. For CCNACA (Fig. S6b), under excitation of different wavelengths, CCNACA shows excellent luminous intensity, and the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths are the same as those of CCNPs, which indicates that CCNPs are successfully incorporated into the scaffold and make CCNACA scaffold have excellent luminous properties. In addition, in order to study the water absorption performance of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds, the swelling ratio of scaffolds was tested (Li et al., 2018; Su et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. S6c, the water absorption of NACA at the time of 1 h (258.63 %), 2 h (306.32 %), 4 h (313.22 %), 6 h (339.09 %), 8 h (345.41 %), 10 h (358.06 %), 12 h (376.43 %) and 24 h (393.10 %) respectively, and the water absorption of CCNACA at the time of 1 h (267.26 %), 2 h (303.94 %), 4 h (307.43 %), 6 h (326.76 %), 8 h (342.76 %), 10 h (367.12 %), 12 h (404.35 %) and 24 h (401.67 %). From the above data, it can be seen that the water absorption of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds gradually increases with time, indicating that NACA and CCNACA scaffolds have good water absorption performance, and the water absorption of CCNACA scaffolds will not be affected by the addition of CCNPs. In addition, we further verified the water absorption performance of the NACA (Fig. S6d) and CCNACA (Fig. S6e) scaffolds by water contact angle testing. By dropping a drop of deionized water onto the surface of the scaffold, the water contact angle of the NACA scaffold decreased from 137.88° (0 s) to 24.59°(40s), while that of the CCNACA scaffolds decreased from 141.62° (0 s) to 21.34°(40s) with the extension of time. The water contact angle was quantitatively analyzed in Fig. S6f, and the results were consistent with the test results of water absorption, further indicating that CCNACA has good water absorption.

Next, we investigated the degradation of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds in different pH buffer solutions. The degradation curves of NACA scaffolds are shown in Fig. S6g. The results show that the final degradation ratio of scaffolds in buffer solution with pH 5.0, 6.5 and 7.4 are 62.3 %, 43.2 % and 39.2 %, respectively. It can be obviously concluded that the degradation ratio of NACA scaffolds in buffer solution with pH 5.0 are significantly higher than those in other pH solutions. In addition, the degradation rate of CCNACA scaffolds (Fig. S6h) in different pH buffer solutions was similar to that of NACA scaffolds. The data showed that the degradation ratio of CCNACA scaffolds at pH = 5.0 (72.2 %) was higher than that of pH = 6.5 (45.2 %) and pH = 7.4 (41.2 %). The results showed that the incorporation of CCNPs did not affect the degradation performance of the scaffolds, and both NACA and CCNACA scaffolds exhibited typical pH-responsive degradation in vitro, which was more sensitive to degradation in acidic environment (Y. Zhang et al., 2020). Therefore, CCNACA has potential application in the treatment of cancer.

It is well known that a large number of reactive oxygen species (ROS) free radicals are produced at the postoperative wound site, leading to lipid peroxidation, enzyme inactivation and difficult healing. It has been shown that the use of materials with free radical scavenging ability at the wound site can promote wound healing. Thus, the antioxidant activity of CCNACA scaffolds is essential for wound healing and oxidative stress in body systems (Liang et al., 2019). In this paper, we determined the scavenging ability of CCNACA scaffolds on reactive oxygen species (ROS) by two methods: DPPH scavenging method and ABTS scavenging method. Firstly, as a stable free radical, DPPH is often selected as a standard method for evaluating antioxidant activity (Wei et al., 2019). Fig. 8a–f shows the different scavenging effects of different samples on DPPH, where Fig. 8a–c is the OD value of DPPH free radical treated by different samples, and Fig. 8d–f is the scavenging efficiency of DPPH free radical by different samples. The results of DPPH radical scavenging rate showed that CCNPs (78.02 %) had higher DPPH radical scavenging rate than CDs (5.08 %) and Cur (61.20 %). CS (10.32 %) and CA (81.23 %) were grafted to form CACA (78.62 %), which not only improved the water solubility of CS, but also had the antioxidant property of CA, and improved the antioxidant activity of CS itself. Most importantly, the DPPH radical scavenging efficiency of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds were shown in Fig. 8c and f. The final DPPH radical scavenging efficiency of CCNACA scaffolds (82.56 %) was higher than that of NACA scaffolds (74.23 %). The main reason is that the addition of CCNPs and CSCA to the hydrogel maintains the original antioxidant activity, resulting in the excellent antioxidant performance of CCNACA. Next, we further verified the antioxidant properties of each material by ABTS radical scavenging method (Wang, Huang, Ma, Huang, & Peng, 2021). Fig. 8g–i shows the OD value of ABTS radical treated by different samples, and Fig. 8j–l shows the scavenging efficiency of ABTS radical by different samples. According to the data, CCNPs (93.72 %) and CSCA (91.03 %) with good ABTS free radical scavenging efficiency were incorporated into the CCNACA scaffolds, resulting in the CCNACA scaffold showing high ABTS free radical scavenging efficiency (96.23 %). In conclusion, the addition of CCNPs and CSCA can effectively improve the antioxidant activity of CCNACA scaffolds, which has potential application value in clinical wound repair.

In addition, ROS has been proved to be involved in cell proliferation and apoptosis. In order to study the antioxidant capacity of CCNACA scaffold in cells, we used 2′, 7′-dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) as ROS sensitive fluorescent markers for visualization (S. Zhang et al., 2020; Thi et al., 2020). Fig. 8m shows the reactive oxygen species of 3 T3 cells treated with different materials (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CA, CSCA, NACA and CCNACA). The results show that when cells are treated with or without drugs in the presence of H2O2 (Control group), strong green fluorescence luminescence can be observed, which indicated that the level of reactive oxygen species in pure 3 T3 cells was higher. In addition, CDs and CS also showed the same condition as control cells. However, weak green fluorescence was observed in 3 T3 cells incubated with Cur, CCNPs, CA, CSCA, NACA and CCNACA, and the green fluorescence was negligible. The above results indicate that CCNACA scaffolds can effectively eliminate ROS in 3 T3 cells, which is related to the incorporation of CCNPs and CSCA. On the other hand, we explored the reactive oxygen species (ROS) of MCF-7 cells treated with different materials (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CA, CSCA, NACA and CCNACA) (Fig. 8n). The data showed that CCNACA scaffold also had good ROS scavenging capacity for MCF-7 cells. To sum up, CCNACA stent has excellent ROS scavenging capacity, so it has good antioxidant performance.

As we all know, good biocompatibility is an important prerequisite for the local drug delivery system. Therefore, we used Live/Head staining and CCK-8 methods to detect the cell growth of fibroblast cells (3 T3 cells) and breast cancer cells (MCF-7 cells) on drug loaded scaffolds (CCNACA), in which the drug free scaffold (NACA) was used as the control group. Here, we selected different dyes to stain the cells. Hoechst 33342 was used as the nuclear stain to excite blue fluorescence at 405 nm. Calcein-AM was used as cytoplasmic dye, and the green fluorescence was obtained by excitation at 488 nm. Dead cells were stained with pyridine iodide (PI) to emit red fluorescence (Y. Yang et al., 2020). Fig. 9a, b respectively show the proliferation test of 3 T3 cells by NACA and CCNACA scaffolds. It can be seen that 1 day after 3 T3 cells were inoculated on the scaffold surface, there was less green fluorescence in either NACA scaffold or CCNACA scaffold group, indicating that the number of adherents on the scaffold was low. After 3 days of inoculation, a trend of increasing green fluorescence was observed compared with the 1 day, indicating that the cells on the scaffold had begun to proliferate and adhere. After 14 days of cell inoculation, we found that green fluorescence covered the whole NACA and CCNACA scaffolds, and the 3D view also showed the migration of 3 T3 cells into the scaffolds. Both NACA and CCNACA scaffolds had excellent proliferation and adhesion to fibroblast cells (3 T3 cells).

Next, we studied the biocompatibility of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds on tumor cells (MCF-7 cells). As shown in Fig. 9c, the green fluorescence of MCF-7 cells inoculated on NACA scaffold gradually increased with the days of inoculation, indicating that NACA scaffolds had good biocompatibility for MCF-7 cells. For MCF-7 cells inoculated with CCNACA scaffolds (Fig. 9d), the green fluorescence did not increase with the prolongation of culture days, and a small amount of red fluorescence appeared, indicating that CCNACA scaffold has a significant inhibitory effect on the normal growth of MCF-7 cells. Then, we evaluated the proliferation of different cells on NACA and CCNACA scaffolds by CCK-8 test. As shown in Fig. S7, on the first day of cell inoculation on scaffolds (NACA and CCNACA scaffolds), OD values were in the range of 0.2–0.3, with no significant difference, whether tumor cells (MCF-7 cells) or fibroblast cells (3 T3 cells). With the extension of culture time, the OD value of cells incubated on NACA scaffolds, whether in NACA-3 T3 group or NACA-MCF7 group, was always increasing, indicating that NACA scaffolds had no cytotoxicity to 3 T3 cells and MCF-7 cells. For CCNACA scaffolds, the OD value of cells in CCNACA-3 T3 group increased with time, indicating that CCNACA scaffolds have good biocompatibility to 3 T3 cells. However, it is worth mentioning that MCF-7 cells incubated with CCNACA scaffold are not always in the state of increasing OD value, indicating that CCNACA inhibits the proliferation of MCF-7 cells. The above CCK-8 data match the live and dead cell staining data. To sum up, CCNACA scaffold has excellent biocompatibility to fibroblast cells and can support the proliferation and migration of 3 T3 cells, while it has certain biological toxicity to tumor cells (MCF-7) and is not conducive to the normal growth of tumor cells.

In order to further prove the biocompatibility of cells on the CCNACA scaffold, we used scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for analysis (Li et al., 2018; Su et al., 2022). Fig. 10a, b have shown the growth of 3 T3 cells and MCF-7 cells on the NACA scaffolds respectively. The results show that on the first day of culture, cells (3 T3 cells and MCF-7 cells) are sparsely scattered on the scaffolds and have obvious cell shape. When the cells were cultured to the third day, a slight increase in the number of cells and a slight aggregation of cells were observed. With the extension of time, the number of 3 T3 cells and MCF-7 cells increased significantly by the 14th day of cell culture, and the aggregation was obvious, showing a spherical shape. As for the growth of cells (3 T3 cells and MCF-7 cells) on the CCNACA scaffolds, as shown in Fig. 10c, the number of 3 T3 cells inoculated on the CCNACA scaffolds gradually increased with the increase of culture days, and the cells were in good condition. However, the MCF-7 cells inoculated on the CCNACA scaffolds grew slowly on the first and third days of culture, and almost all the cells showed abnormal morphology as the culture time extended to the 7th and 14th days, indicating that the CCNACA scaffolds had certain cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells and did not support the normal growth of MCF-7 cells. The above SEM data were consistent with the previous live-dead cell staining and CCK8 data. This phenomenon lays a foundation for the application of CCNACA scaffolds in local drug sustained-release system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Multi-bioresponsive drug release from CCNACA scaffolds
ce:section.ce:para: The study of drug release is the focus of local drug delivery system. First of all, we investigated the drug release behavior of CCNPs in buffers with different pH values (7.4, 6.5 and 5.0), and simulated different environments with these pH changing buffers, which correspond to plasma (pH = 7.4), tumor extracellular environment (pH = 6.5) and tumor cell interior (pH = 5.0) respectively (Anirudhan, Manjusha, & Chithra Sekhar, 2021; Gao, Liu, Su, Zheng, & Xie, 2020). As shown in Fig. 11a, the cumulative drug release amount of CCNPs in the three buffer solutions increased with time, and the release rate was fast in the early stage, that is, the drug release balance could be achieved in 168 h. It is worth mentioning that with the decrease of pH value, the drug release rate increases greatly. In acidic buffer (pH 5.0), the drug release rate of CCNPs is much faster than that at pH 7.4 and 5.0. In the same incubation time (672 h), the final cumulative drug release of CCNPs in pH = 5.0 was about 29.14 %, higher than pH = 6.5 (24.58 %) and pH = 7.4 (12.90 %). In order to further delay the release of drugs and in situ fixed drugs, the aforementioned nano drugs (CCNPs) were doped into 3D printing ink to form a CCNACA scaffolds. Next, we studied the drug release in CCNACA scaffolds in buffers of different pH values (7.4, 6.5, and 5.0). As shown in Fig. 11b, with the extension of time, the overall drug release rate of CCNACA scaffolds was significantly lower than that of CCNPs, and only stabilized when the release time reached 336 h. The longer drug release results of CCNACA scaffolds can be attributed to the cross-linking network of NC, CSCA and Alg. First, Alg forms a network through Ca2+ cross-linking. In addition, CSCA and Alg are chemically crosslinked through -COOH and -NH2 groups, and the -Si-O group in NC forms a hydrogen bond with the -OH group in polymer, further strengthening the interaction in CCNACA scaffolds system (Siboro et al., 2021; Sheng et al., 2021; X. Li et al., 2021). On the one hand, the multiple cross-linking networks formed by these multi-component polysaccharides and nano-clay can effectively load nano-drugs (CCNPs), on the other hand, due to the steric hindrance, the migration of water molecules in the hydrogel skeleton is limited, which affects the release of CCNPs (da Silva Fernandes, de Moura, Glenn, & Aouada, 2018; Shah, Sohail, Khan, & Kousar, 2021; Wei et al., 2020). However, due to the degradation of CCNACA scaffolds, the final drug release efficiency was slightly higher than that of CCNPs. In addition, it can be seen from the data that the final cumulative drug release of CCNACA scaffolds in pH = 5.0 (55.35 %) is significantly higher than that in pH = 6.5 (24.58 %) and pH = 7.4 (12.90 %), which has the same drug release trend as that of CCNPs. In addition, we also conducted fluorescence qualitative analysis by collecting the drug release solution of CCNPs and CCNACA scaffolds (Fig. S8). The results of fluorescence imaging are consistent with the results of above drug release curve, and also provide favorable conditions for the visual tracking of CCNACA scaffolds in the local drug release system. In a word, for the drug release of CCNACA scaffolds, on the one hand, compared with alginate and other natural polymer carriers in 3D printing system (Hosseini et al., 2023; Mirdamadian, Varshosaz, Minaiyan, & Taheri, 2022), the CCNPs sustained release time of CCNACA scaffolds was longer due to the addition of the NC (Wei et al., 2020). In addition, a more novel consideration has been added, namely in vivo fluorescence tracking of drug release process. Compared with liposomes or block polymers as the carriers of nanomaterials, CDs not only acts as a carrier, but also endows CCNPs with excellent luminescent properties (Abtahi et al., 2021). In conclusion, CCNACA scaffolds is beneficial to drug release in the acidic environment of tumor, and has a certain drug release effect, which has a certain application prospect in tumor treatment.

Next, we further studied the drug release effect of CCNACA scaffolds at the cellular level (R. Li et al., 2021). Firstly, CCK-8 test was used to study the effect of drug release from CCNACA scaffolds on cells. As shown in Fig. 11c, two aseptically treated scaffolds (NACA and CCNACA scaffolds) were placed into inoculated cell culture medium for CCK-8 detection on different days. The results showed that the OD value of MCF-7 cells treated with CCNACA scaffolds increased slowly with the extension of days, and the OD value of cells was only 0.26 at 14 days, indicating that the drug release of CCNACA scaffolds inhibited the growth of MCF-7 cells. Next, we visually observed the effects of drug release from CCNACA scaffolds on cells by inverted fluorescence microscopy. Fig. 11d–g shows the live/dead cell staining test of the scaffolds (NACA and CCNACA) co-incubated with MCF-7 cells on the first, third, 7th, and 14th day, respectively. The results showed that the red fluorescence of MCF-7 cells incubated with CCNACA scaffolds gradually increased with the extension of time, indicating that the number of cell death increased significantly, and by day 14 there were almost no living cells. In contrast, NACA scaffolds were not cytotoxic to MCF-7 cells. In conclusion, CCNACA scaffolds have a long-term sustained drug release effect and inhibits the growth of MCF-7 cells for up to 14 days, which is expected to become a new drug carrier for local sustained drug release and tumor growth inhibition.

Next, we used immunofluorescence to detect the expression of apoptotic proteins (Bcl-2 and Bax) induced by CCNPs in MCF-7 cells to explore the mechanism of CCNPs on MCF-7 cells. The expression analysis of pro-apoptosis gene Bax was shown in Fig. S9a. The fluorescence of MCF-7 cells was enhanced after CCNPs treatment, which indicated that CCNPs could up-regulate the expression of Bax in MCF-7 cells. The fluorescence quantitative analysis of Bax with Image J software (Fig. S9b) also showed the same results. In addition, after MCF-7 cells were treated with CCNPs for 12 h, the fluorescence expression of Bcl-2 antibody (488 nm and 594 nm) in CCNPs was significantly lower than that in the control group (Fig. S9c). Then, we quantitatively analyzed the fluorescence data in Fig. S9d by ImageJ software. The fluorescence intensity of Bcl-2 in MCF-7 cells treated with CCNPs was indeed lower than that in the control, indicating that CCNPs could inhibit the expression of Bcl-2 in MCF-7 cells. These results suggest that CCNPs may promote apoptosis by changing the expression of apoptosis-related proteins in MCF-7 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Long-term antimicrobial activity test of CCNACA scaffolds in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: In addition to the efficacy of CCNACA scaffolds in postoperative prevention of tumor recurrence through local drug release, in vitro antimicrobial activity of CCNACA scaffolds should be examined to infer whether they can successfully resist bacterial invasion after direct contact with surgical wounds. In this article, we investigated the antimicrobial properties of NACA and CCNACA scaffolds against two typical pathogenic bacteria: Gram-negative Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Gram-positive Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) (Fan et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2020). In addition, in order to clarify the antibacterial source of CCNACA scaffolds, various raw materials (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CA, CSCA) for the preparation of CCNACA scaffolds were also tested. Firstly, different materials (CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CS, CA, CSCA) were used in the experimental group, and the NACA and CCNACA scaffolds were soaked in the culture medium containing bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus), respectively, while the control group only used the culture medium containing bacteria. After a certain number of days (1, 3, 7 days), a small amount of bacterial solution was extracted for agar plate experiment (Fig. 12 ) (Yang et al., 2021). The results showed that the CCNACA scaffolds showed high efficiency and lasting antibacterial effect, which was mainly attributed to the incorporation of CCNPs and CSCA materials. Next, the optical density (OD600) values of the bacteria were measured at different incubation times (1, 3, 7 days) (Fig. 13a–c). According to the results, the OD600 values of E. coli and S. aureus treated with CCNPs were 0.12 and 0.11 (1 day), respectively, showing excellent antibacterial effect, and no bacterial proliferation occurred on the third day (Fig. 13b) and the 7th day (Fig. 13c). Similarly, the antibacterial effect of E. coli and S. aureus treated with CSCA was similar to CCNPs. These results indicated that CCNPs and CACA improved the antibacterial efficacy of Cur and CS, respectively. For the CCNACA group, in the treatment of E. coli and S. aureus, though not on the first day show the special low OD value, but with the extension of time (1, 3, 7 days), OD value of bacteria were still in the decreasing trend, on the 7th day meet minimum OD value (E. coli was 0.08 ± 0.006, S. aureus was 0.09 ± 0.004). In addition, the corresponding bacterial survival ratio were calculated according to the OD values in Fig. 13a–c. As shown in Fig. 13d–f, the bacterial survival ratio of E. coli and S. aureus were 11.06 ± 0.62 % and 8.50 ± 0.39 %, respectively, after the CCNACA scaffolds treatment of the two bacteria for 7 days, which were consistent with the agar plate experiment. It shows that CCNACA scaffolds have excellent antibacterial effect and has a long antibacterial time.

Finally, we tested the antibacterial (E. coli and S. aureus) activity of CDs, Cur, CCNPs, CA, CA, CSCA, NACA and CCNACA scaffolds by agar diffusion assay (Zhao et al., 2020; Z. Liu et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. 13g, E. coli and S. aureus treated with CCNPs and CSCA respectively showed excellent antibacterial effect, and with the extension of incubation days, a clearer bacterial inhibition zone was gradually formed, and the inhibition range gradually increased, indicating that CCNPs and CSCA had good antibacterial performance. In addition, the sterilized NACA and the CCNACA scaffolds are directly contacted with the agar plate inoculated with bacteria (E. coli and S. aureus), the results showed that the size of the bacteriostatic zone on the surface of NACA was basically consistent with the size of the scaffold, and there was no diffusion with the extension of time. For E. coli and S. aureus treated with CCNACA scaffolds, the size of the inhibition zone on the first day was similar to that of the NACA group, except that the inhibition effect occurred at the position where the CCNACA scaffolds were in contact. However, it is worth mentioning that with the extension of time, the range of the antibacterial zone treated with CCNACA scaffolds expanded. The maximum bacteriostatic range was reached on the 7th day, and the release effect of the drug was obviously observed. Overall, CCNACA scaffolds showed long-lasting, highly effective and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of CCNACA scaffolds in promoting postoperative wound healing and inhibiting tumor recurrence
ce:section.ce:para: The tumor resection model of tumor-bearing mice was used to observe the effect of CCNACA scaffolds on wound healing and tumor recurrence. In our study, CCNACA and NACA scaffolds were implanted into the defect site of mice immediately after tumor resection. In addition, normal mice were selected as normal group, and mice without treatment after tumor resection were selected as control group. It is worth noting that the hydrogel was not replaced during the experiment. Postoperative wound contraction in the control group, NACA group, and CCNACA group was measured on 3 day, 7 day, and 14 day, respectively (Fig. 14a). According to the data, with the extension of postoperative time, the wound area of the three groups (control group, NACA group and CCNACA group) showed a downward trend. It is worth mentioning that compared with the control group and NACA scaffolds group, CCNACA scaffolds group showed better wound healing effect and less inflammatory exudation, which could inhibit tumor recurrence after operation.

In order to more intuitively track the drug release of CCNACA scaffolds in mice, we analyzed the fluorescence imaging of mice with Bruker MISE 721 mouse optical imaging system. As shown in Fig. 14b, CCNACA scaffolds and NACA scaffolds were implanted into the mouse site on the third day after operation. Compared with the control group and NACA scaffolds group, obvious fluorescence signal was detected in the defect site of CCNACA scaffolds group, and the fluorescence intensity decreased with time, and weak fluorescence signal could still be observed 14 days after CCNACA scaffolds implantation. Fig. 14c showed the quantitative analysis of the average fluorescence signal at the wound site of the above-mentioned mice. The above results showed that the CCNACA scaffolds presented excellent imaging performance in vivo, could real-time detect the drug release behavior of the defect after operation, and could be degraded in vivo to achieve the purpose of wound treatment (Su et al., 2020a, b).

The most common consequence of surgical infection is chronic inflammation, which is not conducive to tissue regeneration and slows down the wound healing process. Therefore, the enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) was used to detect the expression of Mouse tumor necrosis factor α (TNF- α, pro-inflammatory factor) and Mouse interleukin 10 (IL-10, anti-inflammatory factor) in mice. It could be seen from Fig. 14d that on the third day after the scaffolds were implanted into the wound, the expression of TNF-α was higher than that in normal tissue, indicating that there was inflammatory reaction. On the 7th and 14th day, compared with the control and NACA group, the expression of TNF-α in CCNACA group was the lowest. It is worth noting that at 14 d, the expression of TNF- α in CCNACA group was the lowest, which was close to that in normal tissue. It is well known that IL-10 plays an important role in inhibiting inflammation. Then we studied the expression of IL-10 in the postoperative site. As seen from Fig. 14e, on the third day, the expression of IL-10 in control group and NACA group was lower than that in normal tissue, while the expression of IL-10 in CCNACA group was slightly higher than that in other groups. With the extension of time, on the 7th day, the expression of IL-10 in CCNACA group was significantly higher than that in normal tissue, control group and NACA group, indicating that CCNACA scaffolds have effective anti-inflammatory effect on mouse wound. On the 14th day, the expression of IL-10 in CCNACA group was similar to that in normal tissue, indicating that the wound had basically healed. The above data show that CCNACA scaffolds have a good anti-inflammatory effect, and then achieve the effect of promoting wound healing (Peng et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022).

On the 14th day after scaffolds treatment, the heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney were all analyzed by hematoxylin-eosin staining (H&E). As shown in Fig. 15a, compared with the normal tissue, no obvious tissue abnormality was observed in the control group, NACA group and CCNACA group, and the postoperative wound injury did not cause damage to the main organs, indicating that NACA and CCNACA scaffolds did not show obvious immunotoxicity or side effects and had excellent biological safety.

Next, we evaluated the healing effect of CCNACA scaffolds on the wound from a histological point of view. Firstly, the tissues of mice were collected at 3, 7 and 14 days after stent treatment for H&E and Masson staining. The result of H&E staining was shown in Fig. 15b, on the 3rd day after operation, the wound site of all groups showed skin defect and a large number of inflammatory cells (Red arrow) were found, but the tissue of CCNACA group began to form scab. On the 7th day after operation, there were still residual scabs and inflammatory cells in the control group and NACA group, indicating that the wound healed slowly and accompanied by inflammatory reaction. However, no scab was found in the skin tissue treated with CCNACA scaffolds, and with the extension of implantation time, the granulation tissue became thinner and the inflammatory reaction became weaker, indicating that CCNACA scaffolds have good antibacterial effect. At 14th day after stent transplantation, a large number of tumor cells (Yellow arrows) appeared in the control group and NACA group, which were closely arranged and had severe tumor cell necrosis. For the skin tissue of CCNACA group, the granulation tissue became narrower, the process of fibrosis tended to be stable, and the inflammatory cells basically disappeared, showing the most similar tissue structure to normal skin tissue. These results show that the wound tissue treated with CCNACA scaffolds have good anti-inflammatory effect and inhibition of postoperative tumor recurrence, and can carry out effective skin repair (Huang et al., 2020; Zheng et al., 2021). Next, we used Masson staining to evaluate the collagen deposition of the wound tissue. It's worth noting that the collagen fibers are marked blue, and the staining intensity indicates the collagen content. Results as shown in Fig. 15b, on the 14th day after scaffolds treatment, the collagen deposition in CCNACA group was significantly higher than that in control group and NACA group, and the collagen fibers in skin tissue were denser and more orderly. The results showed that CCNACA could promote wound healing by promoting collagen deposition (Cheng et al., 2020).

In order to further explore the inflammatory response of CCNACA scaffolds to damaged tissue, we performed immunohistochemical detection. It is worth noting that CD68 is specifically expressed by tissue macrophages including Langerhans cells. As shown in Fig. 15c, with the extension of wound treatment time, the expression of CD68 in CCNACA group was gradually lower than that in control group and NACA group, and decreased to the lowest when the treatment time reached 14th day, which was consistent with that in normal skin tissue (Liang et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2022).

Vascularization is an important process of tissue repair and regeneration, so it is meaningful to evaluate the effect of CCNACA scaffolds on angiogenesis in vivo by immunohistochemical staining. Fig. 15e shows the immunohistochemical staining picture of CD31. The results showed that on the 14th day of treatment, the expression of CD31 in the wound treated by CCNACA group was higher than that in control group and NACA group, and displayed more blood vessels and new skin cells, which was similar to that in normal skin tissue, indicating that CCNACA scaffolds have good angiogenic ability (Liang et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2022).

To sum up, we successfully prepared a nano-therapeutic drug CCNPs using CDs and Cur as raw materials by a simple nano-precipitation method. Local drug delivery system (CCNACA) was prepared by doping CCNPs with Caffeic Acid grafted Chitosan (CSCA), sodium alginate (Alg) and nano-clay (NC) as carriers. The prepared CCNACA scaffolds have good biocompatibility and luminescent properties. In vitro drug release studies have shown that CCNACA stents can be used as an effective tumor therapeutic material for sustained drug release. Secondly, the fluorescence properties of CCNACA make drug (CCNPs) release more intuitive to be visualized and tracked. The antibacterial properties of CCNACA scaffolds were evaluated by in vitro bacteriostatic zone test and Agar plate test. The results showed that CCNACA scaffolds had long-term antibacterial properties. Finally, the animal tumor postoperative model was established, in vivo animal results show that CCNACA scaffolds can effectively reduce the inflammatory reaction of postoperative wound, increase the production of neovascularization and collagen, effectively inhibit postoperative tumor recurrence, and promote wound healing. Based on the above analysis, this study, as an ideal drug delivery system, has opened up a new way for local drug delivery in postoperative wounds of breast cancer.

All animal (Female Balb/c nude mice) experiments comply with the ARRIVE guidelines, and were carried out in accordance with the U.K. Animals (Scientific Procedures) Act, 1986 and associated guidelines, EU Directive 2010/63/EU for animal experiments. And All biological experiments have been approved by the Biological and Medical Ethics Committee of Dalian University of Technology (Approval No.: 2021-065). In the prescribed environment, conduct experimental operations according to the guidance of the Biologicaland Medical Ethics Committee of Dalian University of Technology.
